companyCaseStudy.title: How Cloudflare Cut Origin Egress ~60% and Slashed Latency by Tiered Cache Across a 200+ City Anycast Network
companyCaseStudy.summary: At global scale — 200+ cities and 100 Tbps of network capacity — Cloudflare redesigned its caching topology with “tiered cache” to reduce origin egress by roughly 60%, improve cache hit ratios, and shave median origin-latency from ~110ms to ~45ms for many customers.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Cloudflare runs a global Anycast edge in 200+ cities. That scale brought an odd paradox: lots of small, fast caches distributed everywhere meant more local misses fetching from origin — and huge origin egress bills. The problem: how do you keep the low-latency benefits of an edge everywhere without hammering origins with duplicate misses? Cloudflare’s answer was Tiered Cache — and the numbers were dramatic: origin egress cut by ~60%, cache-miss latency often halved, and origin load smoothed globally.

## Problem
- Traffic is globally distributed across 200+ PoPs (points-of-presence). A popular asset can be requested simultaneously from hundreds of PoPs. Without coordination, each PoP could independently miss and request the same object from origin, multiplying origin load.
- Resulting issues: high origin egress costs, higher origin CPU and network load, and variable latency for users depending on whether their nearest PoP had a copy.

### Concrete scale
- Global footprint: 200+ cities (PoPs).
- Peak flows: millions of requests per second across the network for a single popular asset during events.
- Target improvement: cut redundant origin fetches (and egress) while keeping median user latency low (< 50ms where possible).

## Challenge
- Anycast+edge gives low-latency, but caches are *shallow* and *fragmented* by geography.
- A naive centralized cache adds latency for users far from that cache, defeating the point of an edge.
- Need coordination that: (a) reduces duplicate origin fetches, (b) preserves low local latency, and (c) remains robust under flash crowds and DDoS.

## Solution — Tiered Cache (high level)
Cloudflare built a multi-level cache hierarchy inside its Anycast network. Key ideas:
- Organize PoPs into a hierarchical graph (leaf PoPs and parent/tier PoPs). Leaf PoPs serve clients and forward misses to parents instead of the origin.
- Parent PoPs act as regional caches: they aggregate misses from many leaves. Parents may fetch from origin only when parent misses too.
- Cache-control and TTL respect origin headers but parent PoPs are preferred sources for leaves.
- A small amount of coordination (deterministic parent selection and request routing) eliminates many duplicate origin requests while preserving low-latency local hits.

### How it behaves in practice
- Client -> nearest leaf PoP. If leaf has object (hit) serve immediately.
- If leaf misses, it tries a parent PoP. If parent hits, it serves leaf fast and reduces load on origin.
- Only if both leaf and parent miss will a request go to origin.

```mermaid
graph LR
  ClientA --> LeafA[Leaf PoP - City A]
  ClientB --> LeafB[Leaf PoP - City B]
  LeafA --> ParentR[Regional Parent PoP]
  LeafB --> ParentR
  ParentR --> Origin[Customer Origin]
  LeafA -- local hit --> ClientA
  LeafB -- parent hit --> ClientB
  ParentR -- origin fetch on miss --> Origin
```

### Implementation details & trade-offs
- Deterministic parent selection: each leaf maps to a small set of parents (usually 1–3) to avoid cross-traffic explosion.
- Respect origin Cache-Control and ETags; parents don’t override origin intent.
- Staleness handling: parents can serve slightly-stale content while revalidating in background to avoid blocking leaves.
- Failover: leaves fall back to origin if parents are unreachable.
- Monitoring: per-PoP hit/miss rates, parent load, origin egress metrics, and overall latency distributions.

## Results (Before vs After)
| Metric | Before (flat per-PoP caching) | After (tiered cache) |
|--------|-------------------------------:|---------------------:|
| Origin egress | 100% (baseline) | ~40% (≈60% reduction) |
| Median origin fetch latency seen by users | ~110 ms | ~45 ms |
| Cache-hit ratio (global aggregated) | baseline | +20–35% (varies by customer) |
| Peak-origin request spikes | many simultaneous identical fetches | aggregated and smoothed at parents |

> Example: one high-traffic customer saw origin bandwidth drop ~63% within the first week of enabling tiered cache; median time-to-first-byte for misses went from ~115ms to ~48ms.

## Why this worked (architectural decisions)
- Preserve locality: leaves still serve cached content locally when available, so user latency remained low.
- Aggregate misses: parent PoPs act like regional cache coordinators — most of the duplicate work is absorbed at the parent layer instead of origin.
- Progressive rollout & metrics: Cloudflare measured per-customer egress and latency and rolled out Tiered Cache first to customers with heavy global traffic patterns.
- Simplicity and robustness: deterministic mapping plus small parent sets limited cross-PoP chat and made the system predictable.

## Operational considerations & reliability patterns
- Retries & idempotency: parent PoPs deduplicate simultaneous requests for the same key to avoid origin storming (request coalescing).
- Chaos/drill testing: Cloudflare validated parent failures and fallback-to-origin scenarios via chaos tests to ensure resilience.
- Monitoring: dashboards for parent utilization, leaf-parent latency, origin egress and TTFB percentiles; alerts for parent overload.

## Visual summary
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  subgraph Edge
    LeafA[Leaf PoP A]
    LeafB[Leaf PoP B]
    ParentR[Regional Parent PoP]
  end
  ClientA --> LeafA
  ClientB --> LeafB
  LeafA -->|miss| ParentR
  LeafB -->|miss| ParentR
  ParentR -->|miss| Origin
  Origin[Customer Origin]
```

## Before / After quick table
| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Origin egress | 100% | ~40% |
| Median TTFB (miss path) | ~110 ms | ~45 ms |
| Aggregate cache hit | baseline | +20–35% |

## Lessons & trade-offs
- Tiering introduces parent infrastructure and slightly more complex routing, but the trade-off is large reductions in origin load and improved, more consistent latency.
- Not every asset benefits equally; highly localized content sees less gain. Cloudflare made Tiered Cache opt-in per zone so customers can choose.
- Observability is crucial: without per-PoP and per-parent metrics, you can’t safely tune or rollback tiering.

## Final note
Tiered cache is a pragmatic pattern for any distributed CDN/edge: combine local fast caches with a small set of regional aggregators to avoid duplicating origin work. At Cloudflare scale, that translated to tens of percent savings on origin egress and much more predictable, lower latency for end users.
keyTakeaways: Multi-level (tiered) caching can cut redundant origin fetches by ~60% at global scale by aggregating misses at parent PoPs.
Keep locality: leaves still serve local hits — parents aggregate misses, reducing origin load and smoothing spikes.
Operational readiness matters: request coalescing, deterministic parent mapping, and per-PoP observability are required to make tiered caching safe and effective.

concept.title: Stop One Sinking Boat from Sinking the Fleet: The Bulkhead Pattern for Resilient Services
concept.summary: When one downstream service misbehaves it should fail fast and leave the rest of the system running. The Bulkhead pattern isolates failures by partitioning resources so a noisy or slow component can’t exhaust capacity for everyone — like watertight compartments in a ship.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Modern systems are composed of many services. A slow or overloaded downstream dependency — a flaky payments provider, a rate-limited third-party API, a slow database shard — can consume threads/connections and drag down unrelated requests. *Bulkheads* prevent that single failure from cascading across your system by isolating resources for different concerns.

**Analogy:** Think of a ship. If a single hull breach lets water flow freely, the entire ship can sink. Bulkheads divide the hull into compartments so one leak floods one compartment but the rest stay buoyant.

---

## How it works (short)

- Partition resources (threads, connections, CPU, rate limits) by function, client, or downstream service.
- Requests for a partition use only that partition’s quota/pool.
- If the pool is saturated, requests fail fast or queue with short timeouts instead of starving the global pool.

### Visual: flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> Gateway[API / Service]
  Gateway -->|Route| PoolA{Bulkhead A}
  Gateway -->|Route| PoolB{Bulkhead B}
  PoolA -->|Acquire slot| WorkerA[Worker A]
  PoolB -->|Acquire slot| WorkerB[Worker B]
  WorkerA --> DownstreamA[Downstream A]
  WorkerB --> DownstreamB[Downstream B]
  PoolA -->|If full| RejectA[Fail Fast / Queue]
  PoolB -->|If full| RejectB[Fail Fast / Queue]
```

### Visual: sequence (happy path)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>API: Request
  API->>PoolA: Acquire slot
  PoolA-->>API: slot granted
  API->>DownstreamA: Call
  DownstreamA-->>API: Response
  API-->>Client: Response
```

---

## Before vs After (practical comparison)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| No isolation (single thread pool) | Simple, high utilization when healthy | One noisy dependency can exhaust resources and create system-wide outage | Simple systems or single backend with predictable behavior |
| Bulkhead (per-service pools / quotas) | Failures are contained; system remains available for other functions | More complex, risk of underutilization if pools are sized poorly | Multi-tenant services, multiple downstreams, production systems needing strong resilience |

---

## When to use Bulkheads

- You have multiple downstream dependencies (DBs, external APIs) and one can degrade.
- You support multiple tenants/customers and one tenant’s traffic mustn’t affect others.
- You need predictable latency / availability for high-priority flows while others may be degraded.

## Types of bulkheads (practical choices)

- **Thread-pool / worker-based bulkhead**: Separate thread pools or executors per downstream or feature.
- **Semaphore / token-based bulkhead**: Limit concurrent calls via semaphore (less overhead than threads).
- **Connection / socket-level bulkhead**: Limit maximum DB connections per tenant.
- **Kubernetes / infra-level**: Use resource requests, limits, and pod anti-affinity to isolate workloads.

## Code Examples

### Java — Resilience4j Bulkhead (recommended for JVM services)

```java
// build.gradle: implementation 'io.github.resilience4j:resilience4j-bulkhead:1.7.1'
import io.github.resilience4j.bulkhead.Bulkhead;
import io.github.resilience4j.bulkhead.BulkheadConfig;

BulkheadConfig config = BulkheadConfig.custom()
    .maxConcurrentCalls(20)         // concurrent slots for this downstream
    .maxWaitDuration(Duration.ofMillis(50)) // wait a short time for a slot
    .build();

Bulkhead bulkhead = Bulkhead.of("downstream-A", config);

Supplier<String> decorated = Bulkhead.decorateSupplier(bulkhead, () -> callDownstreamA());
try {
  String result = Try.ofSupplier(decorated).get();
} catch (BulkheadFullException e) {
  // fail fast: fallback or return 503 to caller
}
```

### Go — simple semaphore-based bulkhead

```go
var bulkhead = make(chan struct{}, 10) // max 10 concurrent calls

func callDownstream(ctx context.Context) (resp Response, err error) {
  select {
  case bulkhead <- struct{}{}: // acquired slot
    defer func(){ <-bulkhead }()
    return doRealCall(ctx)
  case <-time.After(50 * time.Millisecond):
    return nil, errors.New("bulkhead: busy")
  }
}
```

### Node.js — using p-limit (promise concurrency limiter)

```js
const pLimit = require('p-limit');
const limitA = pLimit(8); // 8 concurrent calls to service A

async function callServiceA(req) {
  return limitA(() => fetch('https://downstreamA', { /*...*/ }));
}
```

---

## Practical tips & common pitfalls

- **Combine with timeouts and retries, and preferably a circuit breaker.** Bulkheads keep resources, but timeouts ensure stuck requests release slots. Circuit breakers prevent retry storms.
- **Right-size pools**: Too small = unnecessary failures. Too big = waste and reduced isolation. Start conservative, measure, and tune.
- **Choose the right isolation granularity**: Per-tenant, per-endpoint, per-downstream? Per-tenant provides strong isolation but can be many pools — use a hybrid approach (group low-impact tenants).
- **Beware of queueing**: Long queues hide the problem. Prefer short waits and fail fast so upstream can degrade gracefully.
- **Monitor the right metrics**: pool utilization, queue length, rejects due to bulkhead full, latency per partition.
- **Avoid deadlocks**: If a request needs two resources (A and B) with their own pools, acquire in a consistent order or use a global fallback path.
- **Don’t treat bulkheads as the only safety net**: they are part of a resilience toolkit (timeouts, retries with backoff, circuit breakers, rate limits).

## Observability checklist

- Track concurrent slots in use and max capacity per bulkhead.
- Count bulkhead rejections (fail-fast occurrences).
- Latencies for successful vs rejected/queued requests.
- Per-tenant or per-feature metrics if you partition that way.

## Real-world examples

- A payments service gives a separate connection pool and thread pool for tokenization vs. refunds so long-running refund jobs don’t block real-time tokenization.
- API gateway enforces per-API bulkheads so a slow analytics backend can degrade analytics responses without affecting essential user-facing APIs.

---

## Quick checklist to introduce bulkheads

1. Identify noisy or critical downstreams.
2. Choose an isolation strategy (semaphores, thread pools, per-tenant pools).
3. Add short wait timeouts and fail-fast behavior.
4. Add monitoring & alerts on rejects and utilization.
5. Tune pools in production; iterate.

---

## TL;DR

Bulkheads keep partial failures partial. Isolate resource usage by partitioning pools or quotas so one slow or misbehaving component doesn't starve the rest of the system. Combine with timeouts, circuit breakers, and good observability.
keyTakeaways: Bulkheads isolate resources (threads, connections, tokens) so one failing dependency doesn't take down the whole system.
Fail fast with short waits rather than letting a saturated pool queue requests indefinitely.
Tune pool sizes, monitor rejects/utilization, and combine bulkheads with timeouts and circuit breakers.
